flashcards.Back: A hierarchical network design featuring an access layer and a distribution layer. It is also known as a collapsed core architecture and is suitable for smaller campus environments.
A hierarchical network design featuring access, distribution, and core layers. This structure provides high scalability and performance for large enterprise networks.
The point in a hierarchical network where end-user devices connect to the infrastructure. Its primary role is to provide local port density and basic edge security.
The intermediary layer in a hierarchical design that aggregates access switches and connects them to the core. It typically implements routing, security policies, and QoS.
The high-speed backbone of a hierarchical network. Its sole purpose is to switch large volumes of traffic as quickly as possible between different distribution layer modules.
A two-tier design where the functions of the distribution and core layers are combined into a single set of switches. This reduces cost and complexity for smaller sites.
A two-tier data center architecture where every leaf switch connects to every spine switch. This design ensures low-latency and predictable performance for east-west traffic.
A device in a spine-leaf architecture that connects directly to servers and storage. It serves as the entry point for all physical and virtual endpoints in the data center.
A high-capacity backbone switch in a fabric topology that interconnects all leaf switches. It does not connect to endpoints, focusing entirely on high-speed internal switching.
A network design used to connect geographically dispersed sites over long distances. It typically utilizes service provider infrastructure like Metro Ethernet or MPLS.
A simplified network design tailored for small offices or home environments. It often uses integrated devices that combine routing, switching, and wireless functions.
A deployment model where all networking hardware and servers are physically located within an organization's own facilities, providing maximum control and local access.
A model where computing and networking resources are hosted by a third-party provider and accessed over the internet, offering high elasticity and reduced maintenance.
A physical network layout where all peripheral nodes connect to a single central hub or switch. This is the most common design for modern Ethernet-based LANs.
A highly redundant network design where every node has a direct physical or logical connection to every other node, providing the highest possible availability.
A network design where some nodes are interconnected but not all. It balances redundancy and cost by providing multiple paths for critical sites without the expense of a full mesh.
An architectural approach that combines multiple topology concepts, such as a star at the access layer and a partial mesh at the distribution layer, to optimize performance.
The mathematical foundation for the spine-leaf architecture. It is a non-blocking, multi-stage switching fabric that scales effectively by adding more switches to the tiers.
The physical hardware and cabling that provides foundational connectivity. It is responsible for transporting the actual bits between devices in an automated network fabric.
A virtual network fabric built on top of a physical underlay. It uses encapsulation techniques like VXLAN to create logical connections independent of physical cabling.
A WAN technology that provides high-speed Ethernet connectivity across a metropolitan area, allowing businesses to link local sites using familiar Ethernet protocols.
A physical location where a service provider houses equipment to provide local access to their backbone network. It serves as the entry point for customers into the WAN.
The physical demarc point between a customer's equipment and a service provider's network. it is the specific port where the customer's LAN connects to the WAN.
A point-to-point Ethernet WAN service that connects exactly two sites. It functions like a direct physical cable between two remote office routers.
An any-to-any Ethernet WAN service that connects multiple sites together into a single logical broadcast domain, functioning like one large distributed switch.
A hub-and-spoke Ethernet WAN service where a central site can communicate with all remote sites, but remote sites cannot communicate directly with each other.
A network design where the boundary between Layer 2 and Layer 3 is moved to the access switch. This eliminates the need for STP and simplifies network management.
A high-speed network connection that links a lower-tier device (like an access switch) to a higher-tier device (like a distribution switch) in a hierarchy.
An integrated networking environment that combines an automated underlay and overlay. it allows for centralized management and policy enforcement across all devices.
The inclusion of duplicate components or communication paths in a network design to ensure continued operation in the event of a hardware or link failure.
